stories.title: Chesapeake Fire Protection Signs Lease with St. John Properties to Relocate, Expand VA Office
stories.excerpt: Chesapeake Fire Protection, a division of Georgia-based Century Fire Protection, has signed a lease with St. John Properties, Inc. at Ashburn Crossing to relocate and expand its Virginia regional office to the 80-acre business community. The company, which operates two offices in Virginia and Maryland, intends to move approximately 20 employees to a 24,500-square-foot space at 21265 Ashburn Crossing Drive this November. The full-service fire protection contractor intends to increase its workforce to up to 50 employees over the next several years. St. John Properties’ Director of Leasing, Danny Foit, represented the landlord, and Clay Ellis of Colliers International represented the tenant in this transaction. Situated at the intersection of Gloucester Parkway and Loudoun County Parkway, Ashburn Crossing comprises more than 540,000 square feet of flex and research and development space. Ashburn Crossing fronts Loudoun County Parkway and is positioned approximately two miles from One Loudoun and Dulles Town Center, three miles from the Dulles Greenway, seven miles from Washington Dulles International Airport, and just over 30 miles from downtown Washington, D.C. The post Chesapeake Fire Protection Signs Lease with St. John Properties to Relocate, Expand VA Office appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Chesapeake Fire Protection leasing 24,500 SF in Ashburn Crossing, VA; 20 employees now, 50 planned.
Ashburn (Loudoun County) flex/R&D market near Dulles; company expanding operations footprint.
Regional employer growth signals incremental job demand in Dulles/Northern Virginia submarket.
stories.summary: Chesapeake Fire Protection, a division of Century Fire Protection, is relocating and expanding its Virginia operations to Ashburn Crossing, a 540,000-SF flex/R&D complex near Dulles. The company will occupy 24,500 SF starting November, moving 20 employees now with plans to grow to 50 in coming years. The lease signals mid-sized employer confidence and expansion in the Loudoun County corridor.
stories.impact: Limited direct multifamily impact. Regional job growth in Ashburn/Loudoun supports underlying demand, but 20–50 employees is modest. Watch for larger tech or logistics tenants at Ashburn Crossing and One Loudoun corridor as indicators of broader Northern Virginia population inflow that would drive apartment rents and development feasibility.

stories.title: MG Properties Buys Two Multifamily Communities in Gresham
stories.excerpt: San Diego-based real estate investment firm MG Properties has purchased two Gresham-area apartment complexes for $60.8 million. MG Properties bought the Mountain High Apartments for $18.8 million and the Parkside Apartments for $42 million. The two apartments comprise 337 units, reported the Portland Sound Business Journal. HFO Investment Real Estate’s Tyler Johnson and Cody Hagerman represented the seller in the transaction. Financing for the purchase was provided by Fannie Mae and secured by Walker Dunlop’s Bryan Frazier, Blake Hockenbury, Chris Botsford and Jeff Burns. Located at 1930 S.E. Sixth St., Mountain High Apartments features 112 one-, two- and three-bedroom apartments and was built in 1996. Parkside Apartments at 2831 S.E. Palmquist Road has 225 one-, two- and three-bedroom units and was built in 1999. MG Properties has been expanding its Oregon footprint with apartment acquisitions over the last few years. The investment firm has 11 other communities across the Portland region. The post MG Properties Buys Two Multifamily Communities in Gresham appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: MG Properties acquires 337 units (Mountain High + Parkside) in Gresham, OR for $60.8M.
Both assets built 1996–1999; Fannie Mae financing arranged by Walker Dunlop.
MG now owns 12 communities across Portland region; value-add consolidation play.
stories.summary: San Diego-based MG Properties purchased two older-vintage apartment complexes in Gresham (Portland metro) for $60.8M: Mountain High (112 units, built 1996) and Parkside (225 units, built 1999). Fannie Mae provided financing. MG now operates 12 communities across the Portland region and is positioning itself as a regional consolidator of older, renovatable assets.
stories.impact: Signals investor confidence in Portland's multifamily fundamentals despite high cost-of-carry on older properties. MG's repeated Portland acquisitions indicate belief in rent-growth runway post-renovation. Developers should monitor if Fannie Mae appetite for older stock in Portland softens (rate environment) or if cap-rate compression in the region makes new construction more competitive vs. value-add buys.

stories.title: René Morkos of ALICE Technologies: 5 Questions
stories.excerpt: René Morkos, founder and CEO at ALICE Technologies, which is billed as the world’s first artificial intelligence-powered construction optimization platform, is no stranger to AI, contech or proptech. However, he is catching up to the benefits of his company’s new alliance with McKinsey & Company, which was announced in April. A Stanford-trained engineer with a doctorate in artificial intelligence for construction, Morkos is a lecturer at his alma mater. Before founding ALICE, he spent over a decade managing construction projects in more than 20 countries — from rebuilding infrastructure in Afghanistan to automating a $350 million refinery expansion in Abu Dhabi. These experiences revealed to him a core industry flaw: project controls that are rigid, reactive and blind to uncertainty. Under Morkos’s leadership, ALICE (the name is an abbreviation of ArtificiaL Intelligence Construction Engineering) has focused on AI in project planning and scheduling. It’s been used on over $115 billion in global construction to simulate millions of sequencing scenarios, recover from delays, and optimize outcomes. Morkos spoke with Commercial Observer earlier in June about ALICE’s alliance with McKinsey, what it means for both companies and for the construction industry in general, and why he thinks AI is “the bee’s knees.” This interview has been edited for length and clarity. Commercial Observer: ALICE Technologies recently formed an alliance with McKinsey & Company, which usually doesn’t announce such associations. What does this alliance mean, exactly? René Morkos: It means we work together because we have complementary skill sets to push the mission of generative scheduling and construction. We started working with McKinsey more than four years ago. We have slowly gotten closer, and, I think, better, at each other’s respective strengths. I think it’s just been long enough where both parties are comfortable with the other’s skill sets, and, from their sid
bullets: ALICE Technologies AI construction platform deployed on $115B+ in global projects.
Founded by Stanford Ph.D. (AI for construction); now partnered with McKinsey & Co.
Software optimizes scheduling, sequencing, delay recovery across major builds.
stories.summary: ALICE Technologies, an AI-powered construction optimization platform founded by Stanford-trained engineer René Morkos, has been applied to over $115 billion in global construction work. The company recently formed a strategic partnership with McKinsey & Company to expand adoption. ALICE specializes in project planning, scheduling, and scenario simulation to reduce delays and optimize sequencing.
stories.impact: Relevant for developers managing large, complex multifamily or mixed-use projects. ALICE's scheduling optimization and delay-recovery capability can reduce soft costs and time-to-stabilization on development pipelines. Monitor adoption trends and pricing to assess whether this software becomes table-stakes for institutional development teams competing on execution speed and cost control.

stories.title: Mystery bidder on Hawthorne Race Course facility could end planned “racino” development
stories.excerpt: An unknown corporate buyer represented by a shell company is set to purchase the Hawthorne Race Course in Stickney for $90 million, unless another buyer steps in to save the racetrack from likely being closed by the entity.  The suburban racetrack submitted a proposal in bankruptcy court to sell its assets to ALLIMAC 2023 LLC, according to the Chicago Tribune. Previously, Hawthorne president Tim Carey stated that proposals for the site’s redevelopment included data centers, a hot-button issue in city planning across the nation, as well as a $107 million offer. The track is the last remaining professional horse racing […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Hawthorne Race Course (Stickney, IL) in bankruptcy; ALLIMAC LLC stalking-horse bid $90M.
Heavy-industrial zoning; data-center interest; 'racino' redevelopment stalled since 2019.
Hilco contacted 8,000 buyers; 40 conducted due diligence; Hawthorne carries $100M+ debt.
stories.summary: Hawthorne Race Course, the last remaining Chicago-area professional horse racing track, filed Chapter 11 in February with $100M+ debt. A mystery buyer (ALLIMAC 2023 LLC) has proposed a $90M stalking-horse bid, with competing bids due June 30. Potential redevelopment uses include data centers and the previously-approved but stalled 'racino' (hybrid track/casino). The property's heavy-industrial zoning and proximity to Chicago attracted significant buyer interest.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily impact; property is industrial-zoned and focused on non-residential conversion. However, if data centers emerge as winning bidder, this signals competitive pressure on South/Southwest Chicago industrial and logistics real estate that could inflate land costs and redirect capital away from mixed-use or residential development in the corridor.

stories.title: MCB Real Estate Acquires Stake in Annapolis Retail Center
stories.excerpt: MCB Real Estate completed the purchase of an ownership interest in Annapolis Plaza, a 165,565-square-foot retail destination located at 150 Jennifer Road in Annapolis, Maryland. MCB’s institutional partner will retain an ownership interest in the property, while MCB will oversee leasing strategy, asset management, and property management at the center. Annapolis Plaza represents the latest asset in MCB’s established grocery-anchored retail portfolio. The deal further expands the firm’s footprint in the Mid-Atlantic while tapping into consumer demand within the Annapolis area. Annapolis Plaza is home to several retailers, including Trader Joe’s, Homesense, HomeGoods, and Marshalls, with a soon-to-open World Market joining the list. “By securing a dominant center with some of the most attractive retailers in the country, we are capitalizing on proven foot traffic and long-term stability. We remain focused on maintaining this momentum as we continue to scale our national retail platform,” said P. David Bramble, Managing Partner and Co-Founder of MCB Real Estate. The post MCB Real Estate Acquires Stake in Annapolis Retail Center appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: MCB Real Estate acquires stake in Annapolis Plaza (165,565 SF); grocery + retail anchors.
Traders Joe's, HomeGoods, Marshalls; World Market opening; strong foot traffic.
MCB expanding Mid-Atlantic retail platform; food-anchored grocery centers focus.
stories.summary: MCB Real Estate purchased an ownership stake in Annapolis Plaza, a 165,565-SF grocery-anchored retail center in Annapolis, Maryland, anchored by Trader Joe's, HomeGoods, and Marshalls. MCB will manage leasing and asset operations while retaining partner interest. The acquisition represents MCB's continued focus on scaling a national grocery-anchored retail platform.
stories.impact: Not directly relevant to multifamily development. However, strong retail anchors in Annapolis (Trader Joe's, premium retailers) signal healthy local consumer demand and demographic stability that supports underlying multifamily rents and occupancy in the submarket.

stories.title: Newly Delivered Greater Hartford Apartments Trade for $27M
stories.excerpt: JLL Capital Markets arranged the $27-million sale of Blueway Commons, an 88-unit multifamily community in Haddam, CT. Managing directors Jon Bryant, John Flaherty and Steve Simonelli represented the seller, Elm Tree Communities. The buyer was RAK Realty. Blueway Commons, located at 3 Brookes Ct., was completed in two phases between 2023 and 2025. The Class A property in the Greater Hartford submarket achieved 99% occupancy shortly after completion. “RAK Realty was ultimately chosen given their local ownership and certainty of execution,” said Bryant. “Blueway Commons’ rapid lease-up and Class A construction quality in a supply-constrained submarket made it an exceptional investment opportunity in Connecticut’s dynamic multifamily market.” Connecticut’s multifamily market continues to demonstrate resilience, with Greater Hartford experiencing 24% rent growth and maintaining 95% occupancy over the past five years, according to JLL. Haddam’s restricted new supply and strong demographic fundamentals create favorable conditions for multifamily investment in the region The post Newly Delivered Greater Hartford Apartments Trade for $27M appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Blueway Commons (88 units, Haddam, CT) trades for $27M post-delivery; 99% occupancy.
Built 2023–2025; Greater Hartford; achieved full occupancy shortly post-delivery.
Greater Hartford rents +24% / 95% occupancy over 5 years; supply-constrained.
stories.summary: RAK Realty purchased Blueway Commons, an 88-unit Class A multifamily community in Haddam, Connecticut, for $27M. The property, completed between 2023 and 2025 in two phases, achieved 99% occupancy shortly after delivery. Greater Hartford has experienced 24% rent growth and maintained 95% occupancy over the past five years, with restricted new supply.
stories.impact: Strong demand signal for Greater Hartford. Rapid lease-up of a newly delivered Class A asset despite tight financing environment indicates supply constraints and rent-growth momentum in the submarket. Developers should prioritize Greater Hartford for new-construction starts; low supply and strong fundamentals justify development feasibility even with elevated construction costs.

stories.title: City of Redmond Acquires Maintenance Center Site for $38M
stories.excerpt: The Redmond City Council approved the acquisition of a 9.1-acre land parcel from Heidelberg Materials and Aggregates for $38.5 million. The newly purchased site will be home to the city’s maintenance and operations center, replacing an aging, undersized facility currently located at 18080 NE 76th Street. The current operations center serves as the base of operations for about 150 employees and scores of seasonal staff, housing the teams responsible for maintaining Redmond’s critical infrastructure, including roads, sewers, stormwater, drinking water, and more. The new facility will be purpose-built and modernized to improve efficiency, safety and sustainability. “Council’s approval of this land purchase supports the commitment to a facility that can deliver efficient, high-quality services to meet the community’s needs well into the future,” said Council President Melissa Stuart. Design of the new center will begin this year, and the city expects to break ground in 2028, with operations at the new location beginning in 2030. The post City of Redmond Acquires Maintenance Center Site for $38M appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: City of Redmond, WA acquires 9.1-acre maintenance center site from Heidelberg for $38.5M.
Replaces undersized facility; groundbreaking 2028, operations by 2030.
Municipal infrastructure project; no direct residential development component.
stories.summary: Redmond, Washington approved a $38.5M acquisition of a 9.1-acre parcel from Heidelberg Materials for a new city maintenance and operations center. The facility will replace an aging site and house 150 permanent and seasonal staff. Design begins this year; groundbreaking is expected in 2028 with operational occupancy in 2030.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily impact. However, municipal capital expenditure signals confidence in Redmond's long-term growth and infrastructure investment. Redmond's continued expansion (Puget Sound tech hub) supports underlying population inflow; developers should monitor job growth tied to nearby tech employers (Amazon, Microsoft cloud divisions) as demand catalyst for multifamily.

stories.title: Historic Queens Bowling Alley Trades for $45M to Multifamily JV
stories.excerpt: The operators of the Whitestone Lanes bowling alley in Flushing, Queens, have gotten the ball rolling on a major multifamily redevelopment. Midtown-based development firm Urban Realty Partners joined Mar Mar Realty, the private development arm of the bowling alley’s multigenerational owners, to acquire the site for $45 million, Commercial Observer has learned. The joint venture will transform the 48-lane bowling alley at 30-05 Whitestone Expressway into dense, multifamily housing. The 81,150-square-foot lot was rezoned several years ago to accommodate more than 406,000 square feet of residential development, much to the ire of the bowling alley’s devoted local patrons. A conceptual plan approved in 2024 detailed a nine-story development with 415 apartments, but the joint venture’s current plans are unclear. The redevelopment is expected to include affordable housing, benefiting from the city’s mandatory inclusionary housing program, the federal Opportunity Zone program and eligibility for the state’s 485-x tax incentive program. The site will also feature public open space along the southern corridor of Farrington Street and 31st Road. Located adjacent to the Whitestone Expressway, the future multifamily building will reside in close proximity to Downtown Flushing, Citi Field and the massive redevelopment at Willets Point. Whitestone Lanes, opened in the 1960s by the Macaluso family, is a longtime local landmark. Once dubbed the “holy grail of bowling” by the New York Times, the business was well known for its pre-COVID 24/7 operating hours and no-frills style. Owner Marco Macaluso first listed the building for sale for $60 million in 2015 and initiated the rezoning process for the site. JLL’s Mike Mazzara, Ethan Stanton and Brendan Maddigan negotiated the recent transaction directly with the joint venture. “Our objective was to identify a buyer with the vision, capitalization and execution capabilities necessary to realize the full potential of the site,” Mazzara s
bullets: Whitestone Lanes bowling alley (Queens) sold to Urban Realty Partners + Mar Mar Realty JV for $45M.
81,150 SF lot rezoned for 406,000 SF residential; 9-story plan: 415 units + 160-key hotel.
Queens demand supports dense multifamily; mandatory inclusionary housing applies; current plans unclear.
stories.summary: The Whitestone Lanes bowling alley in Flushing, Queens sold to a joint venture of Urban Realty Partners and Mar Mar Realty for $45M. The 81,150-SF lot was rezoned years ago to enable 406,000 SF of residential development. A 2024-approved conceptual plan showed a nine-story building with 415 apartments and a 160-key hotel. The project will include affordable units under NYC's mandatory inclusionary housing program.
stories.impact: Significant demand signal for Queens multifamily. JV formation and rapid acquisition of a prominently-zoned site suggests strong development feasibility and investor confidence in outer-borough NYC rents. Inclusionary housing requirement will compress unit economics; monitor if affordability mandates accelerate or constrain pipeline. Watch for permit flow timing—typical 1–2 year entitlement cycle post-acquisition.

stories.title: Waterton Buys South Florida Rental for $81M
stories.excerpt: Chicago-based investor Waterton has paid $80.5 million for a multifamily asset in Pembroke Pines, Fla., the buyer announced. Called the Landings at Pembroke Lakes, the garden-style property houses 358 apartments, which were built around a 45-acre lake at 10650 Washington Street, about a mile north of Pembroke Road and the neighboring town of Miramar. The deal, which Walker & Dunlop arranged, equates to just under $225,000 per unit. “The Landings at Pembroke Lakes is well positioned to serve renters seeking a more accessible option in the Fort Lauderdale and Miami region,” Casey Mog, vice president of acquisitions at Waterton, said in a statement. The seller, Bar Invest, purchased the 27-acre property for $79.5 million in 2021, the South Florida Business Journal reported, although no deed has been filed in property records. Waterton plans to renovate its new Florida asset, which was constructed in 1989. The buyer took out financing, but declined to provide additional details and the property’s occupancy rate. The acquisition increased Waterton’s portfolio to 1,450 units. In South Florida, it also owns the 321-unit One Plantation, which it bought for $88.3 million in 2023, and the 216-unit Verona at Boynton Beach, which it acquired for $80 million in 2022. A representative for the seller could not be reached for comment. Julia Echikson can be reached at jechikson@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Waterton acquires Landings at Pembroke Lakes (Pembroke Pines, FL) for $80.5M; 358 units.
Garden-style, built 1989; $225K/unit; seller purchased for $79.5M in 2021.
Waterton plans renovation; Walker & Dunlop financing; occupancy rate undisclosed.
stories.summary: Chicago-based Waterton paid $80.5M for the Landings at Pembroke Lakes, a 358-unit garden-style multifamily community in Pembroke Pines, Florida (Fort Lauderdale metro). The property was built in 1989. Waterton plans to renovate the asset, which equates to ~$225K per unit. Walker & Dunlop arranged financing, though specific terms and current occupancy were not disclosed.
stories.impact: Value-add play in South Florida with moderate per-unit basis ($225K) suggesting either distress or off-market sourcing. Waterton's presence signals continued investor appetite for older garden-style communities in Fort Lauderdale metro with renovation upside. Demonstrates refinancing feasibility even in higher-rate environment. Monitor if Waterton's acquisition pace slows due to capital constraints or if similar vintage assets continue to trade at compressed multiples.

stories.title: Supreme Court Rules Trump Cannot Remove Lisa Cook From Fed — For Now
stories.excerpt: President Donald Trump’s attempt to reshape the Federal Reserve toward a dovish ideology that will lower interest rates was dealt a near-term blow by the nation’s highest court. The U.S. Supreme Court ruled Monday in a 5-4 decision that Trump does not have the authority, for now, to fire Fed Governor Lisa Cook, a ruling that comes just 10 months after the president announced Cook’s termination. The court did not weigh in, though, on whether Trump had the ultimate power to remove Cook or other Federal Reserve Board members from the rate-setting Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC). Chief Justice John Roberts issued the majority opinion rejecting the Trump administration’s claim that it had the right to fire Cook for cause without allowing her to remain in office while contesting the decision. Roberts was joined in the majority by fellow conservative Justice Brett Kavanaugh, along with three liberal justices Elena Kagan, Sonia Sotomayor and Ketanji Brown Jackson. “The ultimate question of whether the president can remove Cook for cause will depend in part on the underlying facts,” Roberts wrote. “We have not addressed the facts, as they have yet to be found or analyzed under the relevant legal standards. Rather, we have simply addressed the parties’ arguments about the appropriate legal standards under which the facts must be evaluated.” The Trump administration argued last August that Cook committed mortgage fraud by claiming two separate Michigan homes as her main residence in 2021. Cook, who was appointed by President Joe Biden to a 14-year term that expires in 2038, responded by filing a lawsuit against Trump, with then-Fed Chairman Jerome Powell also securing a preliminary injunction to keep her on the board while waging the legal battle. In a Truth Social post following the Supreme Court ruling, Trump signaled he will continue efforts to fire her, saying, “We will take appropriate action immediately to make sure that someone who has committed wrongdoing wi
bullets: Supreme Court 5–4 ruling: Trump cannot immediately remove Federal Reserve Governor Lisa Cook.
Chief Justice Roberts + 1 conservative + 3 liberals majority; ultimate removal power unresolved.
Federal Open Market Committee composition remains uncertain pending further litigation.
stories.summary: The U.S. Supreme Court ruled 5–4 that President Trump lacks authority to remove Federal Reserve Governor Lisa Cook without allowing her to contest the decision. Chief Justice Roberts wrote the majority opinion, joined by conservative Justice Kavanaugh and three liberal justices. The court did not rule on whether the president ultimately has the power to remove Cook from the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC).
stories.impact: Indirect but material impact on multifamily development. Fed leadership stability reduces near-term policy uncertainty; unclear removal power creates ambiguity on long-term rate trajectory. Developers should monitor Fed composition and policy signals closely: dovish FOMC could lower rates and expand multifamily financing availability, while hawkish tilts compress cap rates and debt availability. Current ruling favors rate stability in near term, reducing refinance risk on maturing construction debt.

stories.title: Sioni Group Acquires 38 West 21st Street in Flatiron District for $31M
stories.excerpt: New York City-based real estate investment and development firm Sioni Group has acquired a 12-story office building in Manhattan’s Flatiron District for $31 million, according to city records. Sioni, which operates a mostly housing portfolio, purchased 38 West 21st Street from Jack Vogel Associates, according to a Monday deed filing in property records. Jack Vogel Associates has owned the property since 1968. Marci Kaufman, who signed the deal on behalf of Jack Vogel Associates, could not be reached for comment. Payman Yadidi of Sioni Group signed the deal for the buyer, records show. BKREA‘s Bob Knakal represented the seller in the transaction alongside Faraz Cheema and Ryan Candel. BKREA listed the 1908-built property within the Ladies’ Mile Historic District as a candidate for office-to-residential conversion. But while office-to-residential may be an option, demand for office may finally be winning out over the trend, according to Knakal. “For four or five months, every single buyer wanted to do a residential conversion, and for the last three weeks of marketing we had five office investors that kept leapfrogging over each other, and it ended up getting very, very advantageous for the seller because of that competition,” Knakal told Commercial Observer. “The lesson from it is that the meaty part of the bell curve in terms of office-to-residential conversion is in the rearview mirror at this point,” Knakal added. “That’s not to say they still won’t go on — office-to-residential conversions have been around for decades and will continue to be around for decades — but the highest volume within that space is behind us now.” Sioni Group was one of those buyers looking to keep the building as office and will be renovating it as such, according to Knakal. Sioni Group, which also operates as Sioni Capital, acquired the building on 21st Street ​​between Fifth Avenue and Avenue of the Americas with a $21 million loan f
bullets: Sioni Group acquires 12-story office at 38 West 21st (Flatiron, Manhattan) for $31M from Jack Vogel.
1908-built, Ladies' Mile Historic District; office-to-residential conversion candidate.
BKREA brokerage reports office investors outbidding conversion buyers last 3 weeks of marketing.
stories.summary: Sioni Group, a NYC-based real estate firm with a housing-focused portfolio, acquired a 12-story office building at 38 West 21st Street in Manhattan's Flatiron District for $31M. The 1908-built property, located in the Ladies' Mile Historic District, was owned by Jack Vogel Associates since 1968. BKREA's Bob Knakal noted that after months of conversion interest, five office investors began bidding competitively in the final three weeks of marketing.
stories.impact: Signals potential shift in Manhattan office-to-residential conversion momentum. If office-focused investors are re-entering bids at scale after years of conversion dominance, it suggests office valuations may be stabilizing or cap-rate spreads narrowing vs. residential. This could reduce conversion feasibility in prime Manhattan locations (Flatiron); developers should reassess office-to-res project economics and monitor if conversion pipelines slow due to compressed spreads.

stories.title: $580M cultural complex in Woodlawn snags Plan Commission approval
stories.excerpt: Around a half-decade after the Apostolic Church of God unveiled plans for the Woodlawn Central $580 million megadevelopment, key rezoning changes are giving an indication that the project is ready to move forward on the city’s South Side. The initial zoning change application for the 8-acre first phase space was filed in December 2025. The new change could allow the first phase, which includes a 14-story residential building with 104 units, to move forward. 22 of the units created would be affordable. The residential building would also include a 160-key hotel, 23,000 square feet of retail space, 3,500 square feet […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Woodlawn Central (Chicago South Side) $580M cultural complex: first phase rezoning approved Dec 2025.
Phase 1: 14-story, 104 units (22 affordable) + 160-key hotel, 23K SF retail.
Developed by Apostolic Church; permits targeted early 2027, delivery late 2028; full 1,000-unit pipeline.
stories.summary: The Apostolic Church of God's Woodlawn Central $580M megadevelopment in South Chicago cleared a major rezoning hurdle, with first-phase zoning changes approved in December 2025. Phase 1 includes a 14-story residential tower with 104 units (22 affordable), a 160-key hotel, 23,000 SF retail, and parking. Developers target building permits in early 2027 and construction completion by late 2028. The full build-out plan encompasses ~1,000 units (affordable, market-rate, and senior) plus performance venue and restaurants.
stories.impact: Significant demand catalyst for South Side Chicago multifamily. Large pipeline of mixed-income housing in underserved, high-poverty neighborhood signals institutional capital and community backing. Affordable units (22 in phase 1, hundreds in full plan) may qualify for LIHTC or NMTC financing. Developers should monitor permit flow and track market-rate rents achieved in phase 1 to gauge South Side rent trajectory; successful delivery could accelerate competing projects and land values in the corridor.

stories.title: B3’s Sydnie + Olivia closes the North Shore’s new construction gap
stories.excerpt: The North Shore of Chicago is entering a new era of growth. B3 has emerged as a leader in the region. The firm specializes in transforming long-dormant assets into the most coveted addresses along the shore. Their newest project, The Sydnie + Olivia, is a groundbreaking 30-unit luxury development that is resurrecting a prime corner of Highland Park, IL after fifteen years of vacancy. We spoke with the team at B3 about the vision behind the project, the demand for high-end townhomes and condos, and their commitment to reviving the region.  Diamond in the rough 812 Laurel Avenue in Highland […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: B3's Sydnie + Olivia: 30-unit luxury (18 townhomes + 12 condos) in Highland Park, IL.
812 Laurel Avenue vacant 15+ years post-2008 crisis; B3 acquired from dormant owner.
B3 specializes in vacant, stalled sites; resurrecting prime Highland Park corner.
stories.summary: B3, a Chicago-based developer, is completing the Sydnie + Olivia, a 30-unit luxury residential project (18 townhomes, 12 condos) on Laurel Avenue in Highland Park, Illinois. The site sat vacant for nearly 15 years following the 2008 financial crisis. B3 acquired the property by directly negotiating with the owner and specializes in resurrecting long-dormant sites in strong markets.
stories.impact: Signals recovery and infill opportunity on North Shore Chicago. Successful repositioning of long-vacant land in Highland Park suggests strong end-user demand for luxury product and improving feasibility of 'redevelopment of redevelopment' deals in recession-scarred submarkets. Developers should scout similar long-stalled sites in affluent suburbs (Winnetka, Wilmette, Glencoe) where land values may still discount obsolescence premium but market fundamentals have recovered.

stories.title: To build or to buy? After uproar over price, Fort Lauderdale city hall proposal back for a vote
stories.excerpt: After an uproar over the price tag and deal terms of a new Fort Lauderdale city hall, a renegotiated agreement with the developer that reduces the cost by over $50 million is coming back for a commission vote.  The city has been working on developing a new city hall for about a year, selecting FTL City Hall Partners LLC’s proposal for a 14-story, oval-shaped building with a sci-fi-reminiscent design in downtown Fort Lauderdale. A record-breaking flood in 2023 damaged the former city hall, and Fort Lauderdale has leased space across several buildings since then. FTL City Hall’s development team includes […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Fort Lauderdale city hall: FTL City Hall Partners (Stiles + Core) renegotiated deal; cost cut $50M+.
New price $217.1M vs. $268M; eliminates developer equity, $12M fee, 11% city loan.
14-story oval building; flood damaged prior hall; delivery timeline TBD.
stories.summary: Fort Lauderdale renegotiated a $50M+ cost reduction on its new city hall project with FTL City Hall Partners (including developer Stiles). The revised deal is $217.1M vs. the originally proposed $268M, eliminating a $12M developer fee and a controversial $24M loan at 11% interest. The 14-story building replaces the previous city hall damaged in a 2023 flood.
stories.impact: Limited multifamily impact; municipal project. However, the reduction from $268M to $217.1M signals pushback on public-sector costs and financing terms, reflecting broader capital constraints. For developers, this indicates city commissions are scrutinizing development fees and carry costs—relevant for any future public-private partnerships or public-sector deals in South Florida.

stories.title: Simon plans “main street” mixed-use project at shuttered Sears site in Boca
stories.excerpt: Simon Property Group, one of the largest retail property owners in the country, is joining the wave of mixed-use redevelopments at South Florida malls. The Indianapolis-based company filed plans at the Town Center at Boca Raton to replace the shuttered Sears site with a hotel, multifamily and retail, the South Florida Business Journal reported.  Simon acquired the 18.6-acre former Sears site at 5900 West Glades Road for $23 million last year and filed the 31.6-acre development proposal, which includes the parking lot, in May. Sears closed in 2018 and will be demolished. Plans include a 197-room, eight-story hotel with a […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Simon Property Group plans mixed-use at shuttered Sears (Boca Raton Town Center): $23M land (2024).
Proposal: 197-room hotel, 374-unit apartment building, 157K SF retail, 8-story garage.
Demolition of Sears; 'main street' landscape; competing mall redevelopment pipeline South Florida-wide.
stories.summary: Simon Property Group filed plans to redevelop the shuttered Sears at Boca Raton's Town Center with a mixed-use project including a 197-room hotel, 374-unit apartment building, 157,000 SF of retail, and an eight-story parking garage. Simon acquired the 18.6-acre Sears site for $23M in 2023. The redevelopment is part of a broader wave of mall-to-mixed-use conversions in South Florida.
stories.impact: Significant supply signal for South Florida multifamily, particularly Boca Raton submarket. Major REIT deploying capital on 374-unit apartment component signals confidence in South Florida fundamentals despite recent market softening. Monitor: (1) absorption trajectory and achieved rents post-delivery; (2) whether Simon prices aggressively to lease apartments, compressing comps; (3) competing Galleria (Fort Lauderdale) and other South Florida mall-conversion pipelines, which could lead to supply overhang in premium retail submarkets.

stories.title: Emergency declaration pushes Philly affordable project forward
stories.excerpt: Intervention from the mayor is finally pushing a significant affordable housing project forward in Philadelphia. The Philadelphia Housing Authority plans to start demolishing dozens of aging buildings in Bartram Village next month after an emergency declaration was issued by Mayor Cherelle Parker, WHYY reported. The declaration clears the way for the razing of 44 buildings, which was held up by a federal review process. The relevant properties in the Southwest Philadelphia complex have been vacant since last year, awaiting redevelopment. Since then, they’ve been taken over by squatters despite the city’s efforts to increase security and board up properties. “This […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Philadelphia Housing Authority demolishing 44 buildings in Bartram Village (Southwest Phila.) via emergency declaration.
22-acre site will be replaced with 514 units of public housing; $425M redevelopment.
Emergency order clears federal review delays; demolition starts next month; residents have right-to-return.
stories.summary: Mayor Cherelle Parker issued an emergency declaration enabling the Philadelphia Housing Authority to proceed with demolition of 44 vacant buildings in Bartram Village, a 1940s-era public housing complex in Southwest Philadelphia. The action will clear the way for a $425M redevelopment delivering 514 new public housing units across a 22-acre site. Current residents will have the right to return upon completion.
stories.impact: Supply expansion in Southwest Philadelphia public housing; addresses structural vacancy and blight in underserved market. While this is public housing (not market-rate multifamily), successful delivery of 514 units signals municipal and developer commitment to Southwest Philadelphia revitalization. Monitor whether this catalyzes adjacent private market-rate development, land value appreciation, or competing LIHTC/mixed-income projects in the corridor, which could affect overall rental supply and affordability dynamics.
